citation.title: Gradient or no gradient: Spatial hydrostatic pressure distributions in bilayer thin-film composite membranes

content: A B S T R A C T Water recovery through pressure-driven bilayer thin-film composite (TFC) membranes remains an open problem, with critical uncertainty centered on its driving forces, namely the hydrostatic pressure gradient, yet systematic investigation remains limited. Here, we present a poromechanics framework that explicitly accounts for the distinct mechanical, structural, and transport properties, elucidating the interplay between flow-induced compaction and water transport in a TFC membrane governing the transport upper limit performance. Our approach naturally splits the strain energy into two regions: (i) a linear-response selective layer and (ii) a non-linear supporting one, mechanically capturing the strain-hardening behavior as the compacted support layer transitions into its bulk polymer state. While the hydrostatic pressure distribution in the selective layer merely scales upward with the increasing applied transmembrane pressure, the distribution in the support layer, however, contorts from a zero slope to a linear one and finally to a non-linear slope, demonstrating how the water transport changes to a support layer-controlled system. Overall, we show how a cancellation between permeance and the hydrostatic pressure difference across TFC membranes drives the transition between (i) support layer-controlled and (ii) selective layer-controlled regimes.

citation.title: Gradient or no gradient: Spatial hydrostatic pressure distributions in bilayer thin-film composite membranes

content: 1. Introduction Pressure-driven membrane technologies, particularly nanofiltration (NF) and reverse osmosis (RO), have become leading solutions for addressing water scarcity due to their comparatively efficient energy consumption in desalinating water resources [1–5]. At the core of these desalination processes are thin-film composite (TFC) membranes, which typically comprise two primary layers—(i) a selective layer, and (ii) a support layer—along with a secondary backing layer. The selective layer, with a thickness ranging from 15 to 300 nm [6,7], is composed of dense, crosslinked polyamide featuring sub-nanometer free volume elements that regulate water permeation and salt rejection [8,9], defining the permselectivity performance of these membranes. Adjacently, the support layer—an ultrafiltration membrane with a thickness of approximately 60 μm [10–12]—is typically made of polysulfone or polyethersulfone [13], providing a surface for interfacial polymerization [7] and structural integrity to the selective layer. Last, but not least important, the non-woven fabric-based backing layer provides further reinforcement, allowing these composite membranes to withstand highpressure loading. While the secondary backing layer is often ignored, being assumed to have a negligible direct role in influencing the

Despite extensive research on TFC membranes, the synergistic influence of the selective and support layers on the flow-induced compaction of the membranes remains unclear, particularly with regard to the spatial hydrostatic pressure distribution across these layers — which drives water transport — has largely been unexplored. As a result, optimizing performance-limiting factors, particularly the upper limit of water flow during operation, remains a persistent challenge. In operation, pressurizing the feed solutions of these membranes generates a transmembrane pressure (TMP), driving water flow through them and, concurrently, limiting water permeation as the membranes compact and densify, ultimately altering their morphology. Thus, this study is particularly important for high-pressure reverse osmosis (HPRO), where the operating pressure (≳ 100 bar [1]) is substantially higher than conventional RO and NF systems. Our TFC membrane comprises a bilayer structure (selective layer and support layer) sandwiched between feed and permeate water, and supported by a rigid permeable barrier at the membrane/permeate interface. These contrasting observations suggest that the deformation may be influenced by factors such as membrane material properties, operating conditions, and the specific interactions between the layers during pressure application, leading to variations in hydrostatic pressure gradients within them at different feed solution pressurization intensities. To simulate water transport through TFC membranes, particle-based methods – especially molecular dynamics (MD) simulations at the atomistic level – are widely used to study the transport and compaction of the selective layer under varying crosslinking degrees, monomer compositions, and thicknesses [17–19]. Fully atomistic simulations, while detailed, require significant computational resources and cannot reach the time and length scales relevant to engineering applications. As a result, they are typically limited to analyzing the nanoscopic selective layer. This limitation prevents the observation of the plateau trend in the flux-pressure curve, which represents the upper limit of water flux, even when TMP is increased to 1500 bar [17–19], far exceeding current experimental TMP levels.

On the other hand, theoretical modeling, such as viscoelastic or poromechanics theories, accommodates the engineering length and time scales required to study the deformation of the coupled selective and support layers, albeit sacrificing atomistic detail. To elaborate, existing viscoelastic models [10,20,21] describe time-dependent compressive strain using spring–damper systems, where the components (springs and dampers) are arranged in a combination of series and parallel configurations. These models enable the prediction of real-time evolution of flux and spatially-averagedstrain in pressure-driven membranes. Conversely, poromechanics models [22–28] excel in quantifying the spatial distributions of hydrostatic pressure and porosity within pressure-driven membranes. These models are fundamentally characterized by a set of continuum-based governing equations that couple fluid flow and compaction phenomena. Generally, existing poromechanics models exhibit variations in strain response and water transport mechanisms. Two notable recent examples include: (1) Fan et al. [28], who adopted a stress–strain response inspired by the Flory–Rehner (FR) theory, emphasizing thermodynamic water-polymer interactions, and (2) Hegde et al.

Here we present a framework that combines the transport of water and the mechanics of SW30-XLE TFC membranes, resolving the spatial hydrostatic pressure distribution across their selective and support layers, i.e., the water transport driving forces, at different TMPs. We couple Darcy’s law with our non-linear stress–stretch model, which captures the known yet often overlooked densification effects (due to the collapse of water-filled voids)—a material property change that membranes experience as they undergo severe (strain-hardening) deformation. While some models capture the exponential increase in compressive stress with compaction, their origin is rooted in thermodynamics rather than mechanics, leading to non-realistic flux behavior at high applied pressure (see SI: Subsection S2.4 for further discussion) [29]. Specifically, we aim to examine a specific scenario involving commercial TFC membranes, focusing on cases where the support layer is significantly more strained than the selective layer; otherwise, additional physics may need to be introduced. Although theoretical models/simulations have been developed for (point) zero-, one-, and even two-dimensional spaces, we adopt a 1D approach to conserve computational resources while mimicking experimental and simulation setups.

content: one-, and even two-dimensional spaces, we adopt a 1D approach to conserve computational resources while mimicking experimental and simulation setups. This approach requires, at most, two categories of two property inputs for each membrane layer: (a) morphology—(i) porosities and (ii) pore sizes, and (b) stress–stretch—(iii) compressive moduli and (iv) strain-hardening factors. With these parameters, we can demonstrate how the hydrostatic pressure distribution changes in the membranes at various TMPs, transitioning from linear to non-linear behaviors. 2. Methodology Our poromechanics framework elucidates the interplay between water transport and mechanical deformation within TFC membranes. We model the membranes as a bilayer system: (1) a permeationcontrolling selective layer and (2) a porous support layer, in which each layer consists of two microphases: (a) polymeric skeleton and (b) water-saturated voids/free-volume elements. Applying TMP across the membranes causes water flow to compact them, reducing their thickness macroscopically and porosity microscopically, which together modulate their transport properties. To this end, our theory integrates non-linear frameworks for both (i) transport and (ii) mechanics to capture the interdependence between the membrane compaction and the decline in water permeability. Next, we explain the governing equations and then discuss the complementary boundary conditions. 2.1.

Governing equations: Coupling between transport and mechanics In this paper, we formulate our framework within the Lagrangian frame, a reference frame that is fixed in space. For simplicity, we coincide this reference frame with the pristine (undeformed) configuration of the TFC membrane. Consequently, the key space-dependent variables in our framework are nominal quantities, which are defined relative to the pristine (undeformed) configuration of the TFC membrane. To reduce our parameter space, we assume unidirectional (i) water flow and (ii) membrane deformation, with these and related quantities depending solely on the spatial coordinate in the thickness direction, denoted as, 𝑋. We begin with water transport, modeled using the pore-flow approach, where a pressure gradient drives the water flow through the TFC membrane. Classically, the Hagen–Poiseuille equation, a representative model for pore-flow transport derived from the no-slip interaction between water and the pore wall, ideally describes the water flux through a straight pore [32]. In this work, we adopt Darcy’s law, an expression analogous to the Hagen–Poiseuille equation that averages water flow over a volume containing numerous tortuous pores [32], to describe the water flux in our TFC membrane.

Specifically, Darcy’s law dictates that volumetric water flux (also known as the superficial velocity) changes proportionally to the hydrostatic pressure gradient, written as 𝐽= -(1𝜆𝑋 )𝑘𝜂𝜕𝑃ℎ 𝜕𝑋(1) where 𝑘, 𝜂, 𝜆𝑋, 𝑃ℎ and 𝐽 are the Darcy’s permeability, water viscosity, local stretch ratio (compressed infinitesimal length/initial infinitesimal length), hydrostatic pressure, and the superficial velocity (i.e., the steady-state volumetric water flux), respectively. Eq. (1) differs slightly from the standard form [33,34], with the correction term 1∕𝜆𝑋 accounting for the membrane’s length change under compression. This correction, consistent with the one by Macminn et al. [26], effectively maps between the membrane’s initial configuration to its true compressed one [35], reflecting that water permeation occurs within the compressed state. To link the macroscopic flux, Eq. (1), with the microscopic properties, we allow the membrane’s morphology to modulate the water permeation. Reasonably, a highly porous support layer characterized by bigger porosity and pore size should demonstrate superior water permeation compared to a denser selective layer.

Hence, we express the Darcy’s permeability as a function of porosity and pore size using a modified Carman–Kozeny expression (derivation in SI: Section S3), given as 𝑘=𝜙3𝑤32̂𝑑2𝑝(2) where 𝜙𝑤, and ̂𝑑𝑝 are void porosity (water volume fraction) and (effective) average pore size, respectively. We utilize Eq. (2) under the closed-packed spheres assumption [36], which inherently limits our ability to completely capture the morphological heterogeneity of the membrane. For example, the support layer comprises an irregular mixture of a (i) sponge-like dense region, alongside (ii) macrovoids (for instance, large finger-like pores) [11,13,21,37,38]. Significantly, finger-like pores in the support membrane would enhance membrane water permeation performance by reducing its local transport resistance compared to denser sponge-like regions [21]. In other words, under the same applied pressure and neglecting compaction effects, a support layer with a microstructure dominated by finger-like pores would demonstrate greater water flux than one primarily composed of sponge-like regions. Undoubtedly, for a morphologically heterogeneous TFC membrane, water flux at the molecular level varies sensitively with local changes in membrane microstructure.

content: for a morphologically heterogeneous TFC membrane, water flux at the molecular level varies sensitively with local changes in membrane microstructure. However, since this work highlights the upper limit of water permeation performance at the macroscopic level, it necessitates an analytically tractable approach that accounts for the overall morphological influence of the bilayer TFC membrane. To achieve this, Eq. (2) represents the morphology of the selective and support layers using just two locally averaged quantities: ̂𝑑𝑝 and 𝜙𝑤. For example, a support layer with abundant finger-like pores can be represented by a large ̂𝑑𝑝 and 𝜙𝑤, while a less porous support layer with a mostly sponge-like region can be assigned a smaller ̂𝑑𝑝 and 𝜙𝑤. This enables us to capture the complex heterogeneous morphology of selective and support layers by just using a single expression, seamlessly predicting the upper-limit permeation performance of the bilayer TFC membrane. It is worth noting that, in addition to the morphology (e.g., pore size and porosity), other physicochemical factors, such as the composition and hydrophilicity, would indirectly influence the transport properties of the TFC membrane. For example, in the selective layer, adjusting the monomer concentrations for interfacial polymerization (IP) — typically m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) — would alter the polyamide crosslinking degree, ultimately affecting the water permeation performance [39,40].

When TMP = 200 bar (plateauing flux regime), the selective and support layers exhibit permeances of 2.32 LMH/bar and 1.39 LMH/bar, respectively, with corresponding pressure differences of 74.89 bar and 125.11 bar. This implies that, intrinsically, the selective layer experiences minuscule compaction-induced permeation decline, as it possesses a GPa-order stiffness (see Table S1). As such, the support layer with elevated transport resistance demands a greater driving force for an increased water transport, which is realized by the steeper support layer hydrostatic pressure distribution, as visualized in Figs. 5(C) and (D). To further emphasize the importance of the support layer, we quantify its contribution to the membranes’ transport resistance by adopting a commonly employed resistance-in-series assumption [15,54–56]. We calculate the resistances of the selective and support layers, 𝑅sel and 𝑅sup, respectively, defined as the pressure drop across each layer per unit flux, following Aghajani et al. [15]. Based on this assumption, the total transport resistance, 𝑅TFC, is the sum of the resistances in the selective and support layers: 𝑅TFC = 𝑅sel +𝑅sup. When the applied pressure increases in the high TMP zone, the support layer imposes the dominant resistance to the overall water transport, with little to no improvement in its flux intensity. Consequently, this resistance affects the driving force within the selective layer, resulting in minimal adjustments to its hydrostatic pressure gradient to maintain a constant flux through the membrane system, thereby compensating for the slight flux changes in the support layer. Collectively, our poromechanics framework demonstrates the interplay between the transport resistance and driving forces of the support and selective layers, highlighting how changes in the support layer extrinsically influence the transport performance of the selective layer. This trend qualitatively aligns with experimental [23,67] and theoretical [22–28] findings, suggesting the presence of a more pronounced compacting stress at this interface. In this TMP regime, in addition to the stress transmitted by the selective layer, significant compacting stress arises within the densified support layer itself (see Figs. S13/14 (C) and (D)). The compaction increases toward the support/permeate interface, causing larger local shrinkage and lower local water permeability. Concurrently, to maintain water flow, a higher hydrostatic pressure gradient is necessary near the support/permeate interface, where water permeability declines greatly. Importantly, the pronounced local compaction near thesupport/permeate interface highlights how high-pressure reverse osmosis (HPRO) processes inherently require substantial pressure intensity from the feed side. For instance, at TMP = 200 bar, the compactioninduced pressure drop near the support/permeate interface accounts for approximately 50% of the total pressure drop across the TFC membrane. This significantly contributes to the support layer transport resistance (see Fig. 6) under operating conditions typical of HPRO systems (TMP ≳ 100 bar [1]), ultimately leading to the plateauing flux regime in Fig. 2.

Nevertheless, it is worth noting that HPRO remains a promising technology for desalinating hypersaline brine, as it achieves higher water recovery compared to conventional RO and is more energy-efficient than thermal desalination methods [1,68,69]. To enhance the pressure resistance of TFC membranes, a stiffer support layer can be employed, though this often results in a less porous membrane [13,38]. Therefore, it is essential to achieve an optimal balance in the support layer’s poromechanical properties, specifically, between pore size/porosity and mechanical compressibility. This balance enables an effective trade-off between water permeance and compaction resistance, ultimately maximizing HPRO membrane performance under extreme applied pressures. Based on Figs. 7(C) and (D), we suggest a multilayered support layer with increasing stiffness toward the permeate side for pressure-resistant membranes. Particularly, a highly porous, less compressible sublayer can be placed near the selective/support layer interface, while stiffer, less porous sublayers can be used further away to counteract the elevated compacting stress. To this end, we have demonstrated how pore-flow-driven water transport impacts the membrane morphology (Fig. 7), which links to the permeance reduction (Figs. 3 and 4) and flux-limiting behavior (Fig. 2).

To provide further context between different theoretical perspectives, we compare our pore-flow (PF)-based framework with the solution-diffusion (SD) theory in describing the permeation performance of pressure-driven desalination membranes. These two frameworks describe the driving force of water transport in membranes with distinct, mutually exclusive emphases: SD focuses on sorption, followed by diffusion down concentration gradients, while PF emphasizes pressure gradients and viscous flow through relatively distinct and interconnected pores [46,70,71]. While both models can quantify the plateauing flux behavior [70], the underlying mechanisms responsible for this behavior fundamentally differ. In the SD model, water transport driven by concentration gradients assumes uniform pressure within the membrane, treating the membranes similarly to liquids in how they transmit pressure. To account for the pressure difference between feed and permeate solutions, the model assumes an abrupt hydrostatic pressure drop at the membrane/permeate interface, similar to a piston mechanism [72]—an assumption inconsistent with the behavior of our system. The model assumes chemical equilibrium at the membrane/permeate interface to facilitate this abrupt pressure change, introducing an exponential factor in the expression for water flux [2,70].

content: In the limit of small TMP, the SD water flux can be approximated as 𝐽SD ≈𝑤𝑤𝐶𝑤 (𝛽𝑣𝑤𝛥𝑃ℎ,TFC )∕𝐿, revealing a linear relationship between water flux (𝐽) and TMP (𝛥𝑃ℎ,TFC). This behavior resembles the selective layer-controlled regime described in our framework. In the context of SD framework, the linear flux-pressure regime indicates that the water concentration on the membrane’s permeate side decreases proportionally with applied pressure. This reduction establishes a concentration gradient, which drives water flow across the membrane. However, with further increment in TMP, the water concentration on the membrane’s permeate side would plummet exponentially, effectively reaching zero in the high TMP regime. In this regime, the driving force of the SD framework – the concentration gradient – would reach its maximum limit. Consequently, beyond the linear regime, SD theory predicts that the water flux would deviate from linearity and asymptotically approach 𝑤𝑤𝐶𝑤∕𝐿. This behavior is numerically analogous to the support layer-controlled regime illustrated in Fig. 2. Realistically, the theoretical aspects of the SD model used to describe the plateauing flux particularly the abrupt pressure change at the permeate interface — lack a solid physical foundation. This is because the membranes, in principle, behave more like solids than liquids, consuming pressure while TMP is transmitted across the membranes [30], unlike liquids.

This compaction significantly alters the local porosity, creating substantial resistance to water transport. We can theoretically illustrate this using Eq. (1), where the water flux is locally dependent on two factors: the pressure gradient (𝜕𝑃ℎ∕𝜕𝑋), and Darcy’s permeability (𝑘). In the low TMP regime, the TFC membrane remains relatively uncompacted, resulting in an approximately constant 𝑘. Consequently, the flux increases proportionally with the pressure gradient, and the slope of the flux-TMP curve is governed by the dense selective layer. Conversely, under high TMP conditions, 𝑘 within the support layer decreases significantly due to compaction-induced porosity reduction. In response, our model self-consistently predicts a larger pressure gradient to drive water transport. However, this increasing pressure gradient also elevates the compacting stress (see SI: Figs. S13 and S14), leading to an effective cancellation between 𝑘 and 𝜕𝑃ℎ∕𝜕𝑋. As a result, further increases in TMP fail to produce a corresponding increase in water flux, leading to convergence and upper-limit water flux behavior.

Collectively, at 200 bar TMP, the interplay of strain-hardening and applied pressure leads to a non-linear stretch distribution, but less pronounced compared to the hydrostatic pressure one (see Figs. 5(D) and 7(D)), resulting in the approximately constant-thickness compacted sublayers observed in Fig. 8(A). 4. Conclusion and implications This study introduces a poromechanics framework that integrates non-linear mechanics with non-ideal pore-flow transport to unravel the complex interplay between pressure-driven (i) compaction and (ii) water transport in TFC membranes. By explicitly accounting for the distinct structural, mechanical, and transport properties of the selective and support layers, the framework realistically highlights their significant combined impact on the overall water permeation performance of TFC membranes. content: s their significant combined impact on the overall water permeation performance of TFC membranes. Based on their individual permeance values (see Fig. 3), we demonstrate that the dense selective layer governs water transport at low TMP, whereas the support layer governs transport at high TMP due to its lower permeance. In the low TMP regime, as TMP increases, the permeation-controlling selective layer maintains a linear hydrostatic pressure distribution, which shifts upward with minimal change in its gradient. In contrast, in the high TMP regime, the support layer undergoes significant compaction (see Figs. 7 and 8), leading to increased transport resistance. This compaction consumes a substantial portion of the TMP, causing the support layer to exhibit a steep, nonlinear hydrostatic pressure distribution. Consequently, at high TMP levels, the TFC membrane transitions from a selective-layer-controlled water transport system to one limited by the support layer. These findings suggest that while current MD simulations excel in capturing atomistic details of water transport through the selective layer of a TFC membrane, they fall short of providing a complete understanding of the transport-limiting mechanisms without explicitly simulating the support layer. This limitation is particularly pronounced under high TMP operating conditions.

content: t-limiting mechanisms without explicitly simulating the support layer. This limitation is particularly pronounced under high TMP operating conditions. Under flow-induced compaction, particularly at high TMP levels, experiments demonstrate that TFC support membranes exhibit strainhardening behavior, characterized by a steeper stress–stretch relationship where stress increases more rapidly than stretch [11,13,61], consistent with our prediction of support layer compaction. Although this behavior resembles the prediction of Flory–Rehner (FR) theory, which suggests an exponential increase in compacting stress with compressive stretch, FR theory significantly overestimates water flux at high TMP, predicting values several times greater than both our theoretical results and experimental observations. This discrepancy stems from the thermodynamic foundation of FR theory, which predicts unrealistically high compacting stress in fully-compacted membranes with very low water content due to the unphysical enlargement of osmotic pressure.

content: unrealistically high compacting stress in fully-compacted membranes with very low water content due to the unphysical enlargement of osmotic pressure. In contrast, our stress–stretch relationship realistically captures the membrane deformation across the entire range, from the pristine state (𝜆𝑋= 1) to the fully-compacted one (𝜆𝑋= 1 -𝜙o𝑤,sup), by transitioning from the mechanical response of a hydrated membrane (water-polymer mixture) to that of a solid material (bulk polymer), enabling the capture of (i) selective and (ii) support layer-limited flux-pressure regimes corresponding to low and high TMPs, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 2. In summary, this study elucidates the flow-induced compaction of a bilayer TFC membrane by quantifying the spatially dependent variables, such as the pressure and porosity distributions, which are challenging to measure experimentally. These findings underscore the individual influence of the support and selective layers, allowing us to understand further the intricate coupling between deformation and water permeation within the TFC membrane. However, it is important to note that the current model does not account for the influence of other membrane physicochemical properties, such as hydrophilicity and composition, which could alter the membrane behavior [7,40,60].
